rules.description: Fuseki candidates prefer efficient corner claims, side extensions, stable shape, and influence only when the whole-board balance supports it. This encodes the proverb jin jiao, yin bian, cao du pi as a weighted prior, not as an absolute rule.
Local reading should score double-atari, atari defense, making eyes, false-eye risk, ladders, nets, and snapback-like captures before slow territory moves.
Shape moves such as one-point jump, knight move, large knight move, attachment, hane, and pulling/leading moves are evaluated by connection strength, cutting weakness, and sente value.
Middlegame move scores emphasize group safety, liberties, cutting points, connection value, sente, and large-scale territory versus influence tradeoffs.
Yose play uses score lead, ownership estimate, local sente/gote value, boundary reduction, ko risk, and pass timing from territory calculation.
When remaining legal plays only fill secure own territory, do not self-fill; pass and enter graph-area scoring unless an urgent capture, atari defense, ko, or neutral yose remains.
Use joseki and fuseki books as priors rather than fixed scripts. Search should compare candidate moves by ownership estimate, group safety, and whole-board value before committing.
Training examples should preserve ownership, score lead, and liberty features because they stabilize move selection better than raw win/loss labels alone.

Robot ranking should read urgent local tactics first: capture groups in atari, save own atari groups, avoid self-atari, connect weak groups, cut opponent groups, and avoid filling true eyes unless the capture value justifies it.
Professional joseki and fuseki knowledge is used as a policy prior, not as a forced line. Search may override it when capture, group safety, ownership, or whole-board value is better.
